preFix.title: Thumb-pivot cut
preFix.subtitle: The short-control finishing cut for spoon-carving
preFix.excerpt: The thumb-pivot cut is the short-control technique for fine shaping and detail work in spoon-carving. The off-hand thumb presses on the blade spine or knife hand, pivoting the blade through a small, controlled arc. A complement to the push cut.
preFix.sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for hand-tool grip descriptions. The thumb-pivot method is consistent across Scandinavian slojd school texts and early-twentieth-century craft manuals.

content.text: The thumb-pivot cut is the short-control complement to the push cut. Where a push cut uses forearm movement to drive the blade across the wood, the thumb-pivot uses the off-hand thumb to pivot the blade through a small, controlled arc. It is the detail cut for spoon necks, fine shaping near the bowl rim, and any place where a push cut would overshoot.
Before you start cutting.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts (blade away from body) and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool is more dangerous than a sharp one. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. Apply firm pressure, elevate, and dress. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
When to use it

Use the thumb-pivot cut when a push cut would give you more travel than you want. Common situations: refining the neck of a spoon where the handle meets the bowl, shaping the tight curve at the shoulder of a kuksa handle, cleaning up a bowl rim, and any detail work where precision matters more than speed.
Push cuts are for removing material quickly. Thumb-pivot cuts are for the last few millimetres of shaping.
What you need
A
sloyd knife
with a sharp edge and a
strop
within reach. A dull edge makes the thumb-pivot cut hard to control because the blade needs more force and the contact point shifts unpredictably. Strop before practising this cut.
The grip

Hold the workpiece in your non-knife hand with four fingers wrapped underneath and the thumb on top, away from the cut zone. Hold the knife in your knife hand with the thumb pressed against the spine of the blade near the heel, or pressed against the back of your knife hand. The knife handle is in a loose fist.
The movement

Place the blade edge on the wood at the point where you want the cut to start. The blade should be at about 20 degrees to the wood surface. Now press your off-hand thumb against the spine of the blade. Do not press the knife hand forward. Instead, pivot the blade through a small arc by pushing with the thumb alone. The blade travels 5 to 15 mm, depending on how far you press.
The chip lifts off the end of the workpiece or at the edge of the hollow you are refining. The blade arc does not travel toward any part of your hand.
What a correct cut looks like

A clean thumb-pivot cut leaves a smooth, slightly concave surface with no torn fibres. The chip comes off in one piece rather than crumbling. If the chip crumbles or the surface looks rough, the edge is dull or the cut is going against the grain. Strop and check the grain direction before trying again.
Common mistakes
Using forearm movement instead of thumb pressure: the knife overshoots the intended boundary. Keep the forearm still and let the thumb do all the work.
Pressing the thumb against the blade flat rather than the spine: you put your thumb on the cutting edge rather than the spine. Always check that your thumb contacts the dull back edge.

Starting the cut in the middle of a curved section going against the grain: the chip tears and the surface roughens. Work in sections, starting each thumb-pivot cut where the grain runs in your favour. On a curved neck, that means working from the widest point of the curve toward each end rather than across the whole curve in one go.
